title: The Salt Cape Where Things Stop Choosing

features.name: salt cairns
features.desc-on-touch: Weathered stones crusted white with mineral, each marking a decision made. You trace one, and the salt crystals fall away—the choice is underneath, but the salt keeps returning.

features.name: tide line
features.desc-on-touch: A rust-brown mark on the shore where the estuary's memory washes in twice daily. Fish scales still glint in it; the archive is still feeding here.

features.name: weathered bollard
features.desc-on-touch: An old mooring post, its paint flaked to bare iron. Once ships chose to stop here. Now the cape has stopped them from returning.

features.name: salt-crusted net
features.desc-on-touch: A fishing net draped between two posts, its cordage fused into place by years of salt air. It catches nothing now but the wind.

features.name: salt shelf
features.desc-on-touch: A ridge of crystallized spray, high and permanent as a shelf, running along the shore where the estuary meets open water. The salt remembers every storm.

inscription: salt water keeps its grudges longer than the archive can forget them; here at the cape they have stopped choosing

file.name: EstuaryRecords_SaltMark_1947-2003.txt
file.note: Coded entries of who came to the cape and when they stopped returning. The entries are encrypted—or they always were.

reverie.title: REVERIE — the cape at salt-change
narr: You're both standing at the point where the estuary
gives up its freshness. She touches the salt crust
on a bollard—it comes away on her fingers white
and delicate, like ash. The tide will choose twice
more without either of you having to say which way.
She doesn't rinse her hand. Neither choice needs it.

exits.label: back into the estuary's long throat
where salt gives way to open water and forgetting
inland, where the salt smell fades and choice returns
along the shore toward the distant rock where the archive once stood
